data.title: Gilt Yields Fall After Autumn Budget Despite Lack Of 2026 Tax Hikes
data.description:
By James Smith, Developed Markets Economist, UK, Chris Turner, Global Head of Markets and Regional Head of Research for UK & CEE and Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist

Chaotic release

The UK’s Autumn Budget is out after a chaotic early release – and for investors, the news is not entirely welcome.

Gilt yields initially fell as initial leaked headlines of the Office for Budget Responsibility’s assessment of the Treasury’s plan began to emerge. The level of headroom – the margin for error surrounding the government’s main fiscal rule to balance day-to-day spending with tax revenues by 2029 – has increased to £22bn, from £9bn in the spring.

But beneath the surface, the story is more nuanced.

For all the talk of a significant fiscal hole over recent weeks, the OBR’s economic forecast revisions only shaved £6bn off the government’s headroom. A well-flagged 0.3pp per year cut in the UK’s productivity growth was heavily offset by near-term upgrades to inflation/wage growth. We’d expected the shortfall from economic downgrades to total at least £15bn.

So a smaller fiscal hole, which means the degree of fiscal consolidation required was more modest, too. While tax hikes add up to an additional £26bn/year in revenue by 2029/30 – 0.75% of GDP - virtually none of this will come through in 2026.

That is absolutely key. Investors had already begun to reach that conclusion after the U-turn on hiking income tax a couple of weeks ago; gilt yields rose by 21bp in the immediate few days that followed, before falling back thereafter. A more significant fall in yields today would have required a greater share of the tax hikes to kick in immediately.

Tax threshold freezes – a key plank of this budget – are already frozen until 2028. Extending them into the next decade will raise an additional £8bn, but not immediately. Changes to pension taxation and a levy on high value properties are similarly delayed. For investors, it does beg the question of whether these policies get watered down - particularly with the next election set to coincide with many of these planned tax hikes.

Zooming out a bit though, the UK’s public finances are set to improve next year – and the Autumn Budget doesn’t change that. Public Sector Net Borrowing is forecast to fall from 4.5% this year to 3.5% next, in large part because of the long-standing freeze in income tax thresholds. Importantly, the government has resisted pressure to increase departmental budgets in 2026 more aggressively, too. Those budgets will increase by 1.8% in real terms next year, considerably less generous than the 3.3/3.4% increases seen through FY2024 and 2025.

Add that all together, and the Debt Management Office has confirmed that it expects issuance to be lower in FY2026.

BoE December rate cut remains likely

For the Bank of England, the Autumn Budget doesn’t materially alter the case for further rate cuts. A more material fiscal tightening in 2026 would have argued more convincingly for offsetting monetary easing.

We do still think a December rate cut remains likely, though. Cuts to energy bills will lower headline inflation in 2026, even if not significantly – perhaps by 0.3pp. That’s helpful at a time when the Bank’s hawks are worried that high rates of inflation today could fuel inflation expectations, keeping price pressures elevated well into the future. But recent inflation and wage data has hinted that those concerns are overblown. We expect three more cuts from the BoE, including one in December, and that view hasn’t changed as a result of the Budget.

That leaves the million-pound question: will the Chancellor have to come back for more tax hikes in 2026? Ever since last year’s Budget, we felt tax hikes this year were inevitable. Next year, we’re less sure.

The Chancellor has left herself with a greater buffer after the fiscal rules – rules which will become more helpful for her by the time of the next budget. It’s an underappreciated point, but from 2026, the Treasury’s rules will allow Reeves to run a 0.5% current budget deficit by the end of the forecast horizon. That will add another £17bn to the fiscal headroom. Unless the economic outlook turns sour, that raises the bar for further tax hikes next year.

Yet the UK’s public finances remain precarious. The DMO projects Gilt issuance to remain close to, or even above, £300bn for each of the next three years. And given the strain on public services, political pressure to top up day-to-day spending will inevitably grow. Current plans have departmental budgets rising by an average of 0.6% per year in 2027-30.

Adding extra cash may well require a fresh conversation about tax. And with many of the smaller tax levers already pulled in this and the last Autumn Budget, it will get harder to raise more revenue without touching one of the major taxes.

Gilt investors were satisfied today, but fiscal concerns not over

The knee-jerk reaction in Gilt yields just after the leaking of the OBR report and also during earlier Budget rumours underline the markets’ sensitivity to the UK’s fiscal outlook. With the 10Y Gilt yield a few basis points lower in the aftermath, investors seem reasonably content with the Chancellor’s efforts to contain the government’s debt.

Part of the nudge lower in Gilt yields may also be due to investors’ positioning going into today, given there was a non-negligible risk that the Budget would fail to show credibility. For now there is some relief, but as mentioned above, we may very well face more uncertainty again in the near future.

At time of writing, Gilt yields are currently off around 4-5bp across the curve from their pre-OBR leak levels and EUR/GBP is off around 0.4%. At above 1.32, GBP/USD is back to levels seen in late October.

Over recent weeks Gilts and sterling have been pushed around by both fiscal credibility issues and by what today’s Budget could mean for BoE policy. While Gilt investors could have shown concern over the back-loaded nature of fiscal consolidation, instead they seemed to have backed Chancellor Reeves and the OBR’s path for narrower budget deficits over the next five years.

We had estimated that there was around a 10bp risk premium priced into Gilts ahead of this Budget, suggesting there could be a little more upside for Gilts from current levels. Helping the story as well has been the DMO’s Gilt issuance plans of £304bn and £275bn for '25/26 and '26/27 respectively, broadly as expected.

As for sterling, there had not been too much risk priced into the pound, but today’s Budget is not particularly contractionary in the short term and has not caused a significant repricing of the BoE terminal rate. This has provided a little support. Remember that it was the prospect of big income tax hikes and what it could mean for the BoE policy in 2026 which had hit sterling a couple of weeks ago.

We doubt sterling needs to rally too much further from here, however. We still look for the BoE to cut rates in December and expect the policy rate to be cut a little more quickly to the 3.25% area than the market currently prices. We expect EUR/GBP to find good support ahead of 0.87 and still favour a move towards 0.90 next year.

Perhaps one of the biggest turnarounds today has been the FTSE 100. This is up close to 1% from intraday lows led by financials. The two potential positives here are the Chancellor going ahead with plans to encourage ISAs to be used for investment purposes from 2027 onwards and the rumoured Stamp Duty Reserve Tax exemptions for UK listings over the next three years. These moves will be welcomed by the City, but a more durable rally in UK equities will surely hang on UK growth prospects – which still look unspectacular.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: YPF: Argentina Finally Turns A Corner And So Does Its Flagship Company (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
A story that came back unexpectedly

Today I’m going to analyze YPF (YPF) again, and every time I sit down to write about it, it seems like the first time I do. Quarter by quarter I see great changes, from a huge company that, despite the glitches, continues to advance... Undoubtedly YPF has a course, and that’s the only thing that matters.

Over the past year I have seen different stages of YPF. First, I saw it as the most obvious opportunity within the Argentine equity market; then moved to a hold, since the strong rise of the stock led me to think that much of the initial revaluation was already incorporated. Finally, in my previous coverage I observed that deep value had been left behind and that the thesis had transformed into an execution case, together to the volatile context characteristic of the Argentine economy in pre-election periods.

After reviewing in detail the Q3, the operational leap of the shale in October and November, the new productive map after the exit of the conventional, the international financing that reappeared and the political change that reconfigured the perception of country risk, I feel the story is changing again. YPF is no longer a company seeking to stabilize itself, but a company that is accelerating its operational scale and strategic profile.

Many events have occurred in the last two and a half months since the last analysis. And not only from the point of view of the company, which we will see later in more detail, but also in the country. President Milei’s legislative victory immediately rearranged the macro map, to such an extent that the market reacted as it had not for a long time. Going back to the pre-market post-election Monday, YPF went up 25%, banks 30%, utilities over 20%, and for the first time in many years, Argentina received an explicit message of financial support: the US Treasury confirmed a swap for $20B and a direct endorsement linked to the election result.

Why is all this so important? Because it helps us remember that YPF is a publicly owned company, and has historically been "hand-cuffed" by government regulation, controls and macro volatility. So, in companies so sensitive to these changes, when policy and macro stabilizes, it is a breath of fresh air for YPF. And this "fresh air" comes just as the company is implementing its biggest projects of recent years, which require clear rules, stability and global financing, and which are only now becoming viable.

In addition, the results of Q3, combined with several opportunities developed in recent months, which we will discuss shortly, lead me to upgrade my perspective on YPF, and return to where I started in April. Today I am seeing a company that scales, accelerates, performs well, and for the first time in several years finds macro winds at its back. And that changes my rating to buy.

A business that becomes more efficient, bigger and faster

Looking at the Q3 results in isolation, I had the impression that it was modest as revenues were $4.6B, an 11% YoY drop explained by lower Brent price and a negative FCF of $759MM mainly explicable by capex intensity. But when I analyzed them in continuity with previous quarters and the context, the reading changed: behind these data appears a company that is not in a normal quarter, but in full profound transformation.

Total production was 523 kboe/d, affected by the departure of conventional oil, which is redefining the country’s oil map. However, this exit is strategic, since the operational core of YPF is now explicitly shale. This shows that it produced 170 kbbl/d in Q3 (+35% YoY), rose to 190 kbbl/d in October and exceeded 200,000 barrels per day in November. Undoubtedly, this pace is not explained only by investment; it is explained by a different way of operating.

In terms of costs, according to YPF figures the lifting cost fell to $9 and in areas like La Angostura Sur already around $5, with breakevens below $40, which marks a structural change in competitiveness.

The downstream also accompanied this transformation. The La Plata refinery operated at 97%, its highest level since 2009, was recognized as “Refinery of the Year” and even allowed the company to export refined fuels.

At the same time, the company did not neglect the downstream. In this regard I must highlight a fact that clearly shows the moment the company is going through: the refinery of La Plata operated at 97%, its maximum since 2009, was recognized as "Refinery of the Year" and allowed to export refined fuels.

Finally, YPF is reconfiguring its portfolio, disengaging from mature blocks to definitively close the stage of assets that drained capital. Phase 2 of the Andes Project includes iconic assets such as Manantiales Behr and Chachahuén Sur, with decades of history in the Argentine industry. For me, this is more than a sale, it’s confirmation of a radical strategic change, as I mentioned in the introduction: YPF wants to be known for shale, wants to be an exporter and wants to finance scale-changing infrastructure. And today it can do so because, for the first time in many years, it has an accompanying macro and regulatory context.

A political and financial climate that reduced country risk and opened up new opportunities

In this section I will analyze several "milestones" of the last two and a half months, both from a political and economic point of view and exclusively linked to YPF. However, because of the characteristics of the company itself, I think it is better understood where it stands today if we look at the whole story.

Let’s start with the election results. After the defeat in the Province of Buenos Aires, risk retook the market and country risk rose to levels not seen since Milei’s assumption. Since the government saw what was happening and also perceived everything that was at stake, they decided to double down and play strong in the legislative elections of October, finally achieving a loose victory.

The reaction to this outcome was immediate: country risk was reduced by almost half in days. The market realized that the economic agenda would continue, that second-generation reforms would move forward and that the energy industry could enter an expansive phase that transcended a single government.

A decisive factor was the announcement of the $20B swap with the US days before the election, which reinforced that reading and reduced the likelihood of extreme scenarios. For a company which has been conditioned for years by unstable regulations, this stabilization is not a passing tailwind but a change of operating regime. Even the legal front was accompanied by a positive nuance: although the Burford case remains open, the order to surrender shares was suspended.

With the population validating the macroeconomic course at the polls, the long-term outlook finally begins to open again. And when the macro emerges, finances begin to settle. In the case of YPF, for the first time in years it returned to the international credit market on competitive terms: it closed an overwritten loan for $700MM, reopened the 2031 bond at the lowest cost of the last eight years and got banks from Asia and the US to participate in the operation.

None of this is casual. In capital-intensive firms such as YPF, access to global finance defines investment rates and execution capacity. I believe that, if Argentina maintains political and regulatory stability, YPF will once again position itself as a viable issuer for the international market, something which was unthinkable just twelve months ago.

This new context made it possible to advance what I consider the most important structural announcement of recent years: the framework agreement between YPF, ENI and XRG, the investor arm of ADNOC, to move forward in the 12-MTPA phase of the Argentina LNG project. The entry of a Gulf player is, for me, a technical validation of the resource, a confirmation that the current regulatory framework allows to think projects to 2030 and the evidence that Vaca Muerta has the scale to insert itself in the global gas map. The potential to reach 30 MTPA by the end of the decade completely changes the history of the company and the country.

In parallel with this new macro and strategic window, the company has accelerated its operations more than expected. In November, YPF exceeded 200,000 barrels of shale oil per day for the first time in its history. Less than two years ago it produced 110,000, which implies an increase of 82% that is not only explained by capex, but by a change in the way of operating: autonomous drilling, predictive algorithms, real-time analysis and a level of efficiency that today competes with the Permian.

Meanwhile, YPF continues to adjust its strategy, and in this line advances in the reconfiguration of its portfolio. According to the latest news, the Andes Project has already transferred 46 conventional areas and Phase 2 adds another 16, including historical sites such as Manantiales Behr and Chachahuén Sur. This shows that YPF is focusing all its efforts on what defines its future: shale, exports and infrastructure projects with the capacity to scale the business, leaving behind assets that drained capital and offered low structural profitability.

What we see, in summary, is an unusual convergence for Argentina: a macro that stops playing against it, financing that reappears, a global partner that validates the LNG opportunity, a record-setting operation and a strategy that is ultimately ordered around the highest return assets. YPF is not simply growing; it is redefining its scale and competitive profile at a time when the country, for the first time in years, begins to function as a backup rather than a limiter.

Risks still to be kept in mind

Even in this much more favourable setup, important risks remain. First, I must point out that the Argentine macro is still fragile because reforms have yet to be implemented and sustained over time, and this process is not guaranteed. Any regulatory or political setback may affect fuel parities, access to foreign exchange and ability to move prices, a key point for a company so exposed to the domestic market.

On the other hand, we also observe that YPF continues to show negative FCF for capex intensity, even with access to global financing. Total debt stands at $11.2B, which requires the macro wind to continue blowing from the right side as an abrupt change in rates, country risk or external conditions could strain its financial landscape.

Operationally, I think the complete transition to shale requires absorbing accelerated growth without losing efficiency. Managing such a fast learning curve, consolidating teams, maintaining productivity and keeping lifting costs low is a real challenge. In addition, it is also important to remember that the Burford case remains a latent legal risk, even though it is suspended for now, and an adverse failure could generate costs to the company or to the Argentine state.

Finally, the global volatility of Brent can always modify the shale equation, even with the competitive lifting costs that YPF currently presents. A prolonged low-price scenario would cut margins and limit growth, especially in years of intensive capex.

Conclusions: why today is Buy

After a thorough review of Q3 results, the shale operational jump, the new productive map, international financing, Milei’s political victory and a more predictable regulatory scenario, I am convinced that YPF is entering a different cycle. It’s not the same company I analyzed six months ago, and definitely not the same one that the market still seems to be looking at.

I have no doubt that YPF is operating better than at any other recent time, as it produces more, with better costs, with an efficiency unknown until a few years ago and with a strategy that, for the first time in decades, is really aligned with the highest return assets. It regained access to international credit, managed to attract partners who validate its scale and opened the door to the most ambitious LNG project in the country’s history. These milestones rarely happen together in Argentina, and for me they completely change the reading of risk and potential.

The multiples, however, still tell another story: P/E drops from 14.22x in 2025 to 8.82x in 2026 and 6.85x in 2027, EV/EBITDA FWD is 4.9x and P/CF FWD is just 2.56x, which makes me think that the market still values YPF as operating under the old restrictions, with interventionist prices, high costs, no access to global financing and with a macro that always played against it. But that YPF is falling behind. What is now emerging is a record-breaking shale company, improving its structural competitiveness, shedding capital-draining assets, financing global scale infrastructure and, for the first time in many years, benefits from a regulatory framework that is no longer an obstacle to becoming an enabler.

For all that I raise my recommendation to Buy. Not because the price has changed, nor because the market has reacted to the election result, but because, this time, the operation, the macro, the financing and the strategy were aligned at the same time. From my point of view, YPF is entering a new cycle – clearer, more efficient and larger. And honestly, after many years following their story, I think this time the market came too late.

data.title: Dell Still Screens Margin Risk (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL) shares are in the green pre-market on its Q3 results reported after the bell. Sales were down sequentially by about 9% with Infrastructure Solutions Group or ISG generating about $14.1B in sales, down 16% Q/Q from $16.8B reported last quarter. Clearly, this is not what markets are excited about; instead, that would be the raised annual outlook for its AI servers from $20B to $25B for the current year. This is a big bang for the world of AI, considering markets have been red for the greater part of November, mimicking an early April-like selloff over panic that the AI bubble is popping and circular financing. The market’s reaction to Dell’s raised AI server outlook confirms that there are still those willing to bite on AI in this market, confirming our thesis that we’re not amid a bubble pop, we’re just watching a bubble deflation.

Taking a closer look at Dell, however, we see trouble ahead: margin pressure increasing into 2026 and more hyperscalers going straight to Taiwanese ODMs, skipping the middle man that is Dell. We unpack these two ideas below, and they are the reason we’re downgrading Dell to a Sell.

Margins aren’t safe for long: costs going up, plus hyperscalers going straight to ODM

This quarter’s expanded gross margins, a big point of concern for Dell and its peers like Super Micro (SMCI), did much better sequentially, rising from 18.3% last quarter to 21.1% in Q3, although margins are still down from a year ago. The chart below showcases Dell’s revenue and gross margins across the past few quarters, and a trend becomes obvious: since Q1 FY26, margins have been under pressure. Dell’s gross margin pressure is the result of extremely heavy competition within AI server supply chain; keep in mind that when the entire AI boom began, guys like Dell and SMCI were the main industry go-tos, but we’ve seen the competitive landscape mature a lot since those so-called early days.

Increased competition by default spells increased pricing pressure. So, the likes of Dell, SMCI, and even Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE) need to bring down their price in order to remain competitive. This is an overarching theme weighing down margins, and the closer we look, the more risk we see around margins even after this sequential recovery.

The chart below showcases Dell’s infrastructure solutions group's operating margin, which is arguably the most important place to watch when it comes to Dell because the stock is trading on AI upside, rather than, say, a strong PC recovery. This quarter, margin for this unit came in at 12.4%, better than the expected 11.2%.

This quarter, Dell shipped $5.6B in servers with a remaining backlog of $18.4B. This comes in contrast to Q2, during which Dell ended the quarter with $8.2B shipped and a backlog of $11.7B. Part of why we’re seeing uneven server sales for Dell and even SMCI, which pushed a big chunk of its anticipated Q1 revenue to Q2 due to one large-scale customer requesting configuration changes to GPU racks. Nvidia is ramping its GB300/B300, and this is a more complex product with higher density and power consumption, requiring increased components such as cold plates and an overall cooling system.

So, we think Dell could be struggling with technical requirements of the new product ramp as well into next year. If our thesis on this front is correct, then we’re also dealing with potential risk around Dell’s Q4 guidance.

These headwinds to gross margin expansion are well acknowledged across the industry; simply put, they are not new. What is new, however, is the fact that we’re seeing hyperscalers and tier 1 players skip the middleman and go directly for Taiwanese ODMs, which have lower margins to sustain. OpenAI’s (OPENAI) Sam Altman did just that this quarter after news that Foxconn and OpenAI announced a strategic partnership to build next-gen AI infrastructure in the U.S. Foxconn is the prime poster face for Taiwanese ODMs, making Nvidia’s AI GPUs, plus assembling most iPhones worldwide. We think this increases the risk of hyperscalers cutting down on costs by going straight to the source.

In addition to the increased competition, Dell and other semis are dealing with supply constraints and higher costs, mainly on the memory front. Just last week, DRAM average spot prices were up across the board, with some DRR4 and DRR5 chips seeing prices increase 158% and 307% since the start of September. On the Q3 earnings call, management noted,

“the fact is the cost basis is going up across all products. No one more unique than others. Everything uses a CPU, has DRAM, has storage in it.”

COO Jeff Clarke explained during Q&A that:

…we're in a very unique time. It's unprecedented. We have not seen costs move at the rate that we've seen. And by the way, it's not unique to DRAM. It's NAND. It is hard drives, leading-edge nodes across the semiconductor network. There is a -- if you will, I'd categorize it as demand is way ahead of supply.

Management cost outlook for Q4 remains “largely unchanged,” and that concerns us because it creates a bubble of risk in the case that prices continue to climb this current quarter, potentially impacting Q4 guide. When input costs go up, Dell is usually able to recover two-thirds of that cost within a 90-day period, but that’s during normal times, and management clearly stated: “This is not normal times.” So, higher costs heading into Q4 lead us to believe that gross margin improvement is not a for sure thing in the current environment.

Bottom line:

Dell still looks cheap on paper, but the forward setup doesn’t justify holding it long, in our opinion. The stock trades at a forward P/E of 13.21x, lower than the group average of 23.23x, and a forward EV/Sales of deeply discounted from its group average as well. Still, we don't think the price justifies the higher risk ahead.

The market is pricing Dell this way for a reason: margins are starting to show improvement, AI momentum remains, and management just reported a better-than-expected quarter that, to me, marks the top of the operating leverage cycle. Dell shares are holding up YTD, rising about 8%. The stock got hammered over the past month, down around 22% dropping from its all-time high in October of $168 per share. RSI got dragged down with the broader tech group during the November selloff to 37, escaping oversold territory a couple of days before earnings.

Looking ahead, management is guiding for sales at $31.5B comfortably ahead of estimates for $27.98B, with the bulk of growth expected to come from ISG estimated to grow in about 65% Y/Y, with AI server shipments of $9.4B. We understand that Dell looks very good against a shaky AI backdrop, but we don’t see substantial room for Dell to outperform the SOXX benchmark in 1H26. Therefore, with a tougher margin setup ahead and the bar now higher after such a print, we’re downgrading DELL stock to a Sell.

data.title: 3 Major Potential Headwinds For 2026
data.description:
It is difficult to believe we are already at Thanksgiving Day in 2025. Where does the time go? The markets have continued to rally as they have since late 2022, with the debut of ChatGPT. The market had one big, but very brief sell-off in the first half of April on the announcement of "reciprocal tariffs." This pullback was short-lived, and equities are not that far off from all-time highs.

Most of the gains in the overall market for the year have once again been powered by the AI-related megacaps like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Alphabet (GOOG) that have larger market capitalizations than the annual GDPs of either France or the U.K. Valuation metrics, viewed from a historical lens, have been ignored throughout 2025. There has been increasing chatter around a potential AI bubble, which has triggered some slight short-term moves to the downside. But these dips have been largely bought throughout the year.

So, outside of valuations and the AI Narrative collapsing in 2026, what could bring the market down in the year ahead? Today's article highlights three potential developments prudent investors should pay close attention to as we head into the New Year.

1. Major Buybacks Ebb

Barrels of ink have been spilled recently around a potential AI bubble forming. More and more pundits are also calling attention to the vendor-financing-circle that is driving the AI Narrative to a large extent. It is reminiscent of the vendor financing provided by the likes of Cisco Systems (CSCO) late in the Internet Boom.

However, one aspect of the massive surge of tech spending around the AI infrastructure has been little discussed to this point. Amazon's (AMZN) capital budget in FY2026 will be roughly three times that of FY2023. All the major tech giants have ratcheted up their capital spending as well.

This spending can largely no longer be financed entirely from free cash flow or cash on balance sheets. Amazon, Meta Platforms (META), and Oracle (ORCL) have all executed major recent bond deals to raise additional funding for this buildout. Even special purpose vehicles or SPVs have also made a significant return to the market.

One of the casualties of this huge increase in capital spending will be stock buybacks. And that has contributed nicely to earnings growth in recent years, and this is mitigating dilution from the large stock and options the tech giants grant their employees. In the five years through the end of the third quarter, Apple (AAPL), NVIDIA, Microsoft, Oracle, Meta, and Alphabet have spent a combined $1.1 trillion on stock repurchases. As my late father would say, that is "real money." A significant reduction in the pace of stock buyback activity will negatively impact EPS growth, as will interest on debt being taken on to fund this AI infrastructure buildout. Amazon stopped its stock repurchases in 2022, it should be noted.

2. Real Estate Continues To Head South

I recently highlighted again why the pain is just beginning in residential real estate. Reasons for this pessimism include housing affordability remaining near historical lows and the highest number of home sellers versus home buyers in more than a decade.

Home prices are finally starting to come down in many metro areas, and we still have a long way to go before home affordability gets back down even close to its historical average.

Then, we have the continued deterioration in the Commercial Real Estate or CRE market, which has some $4.8 trillion in debt outstanding.

The delinquency rate on Commercial Mortgage Back Securities or CMBS against office properties (approximately $1.3 trillion of debt outstanding overall) is already above that of the peak of the Great Financial Crisis. And this does not consider any "extend and pretend" loan modifications that have been made by lenders.

Then we have the rapidly deteriorating multi-family space (approximately $2.2 trillion in debt outstanding overall). CMBS delinquency rates have more than doubled in just over a year to right at seven percent.

In addition, rents across the nation are starting to flatline and have started to fall in many metro areas. More new apartments came online in 2024 than in any year since 1973, and over 2 million recent migrants have left the United States so far in 2025.

Downward pressure on rents while costs (insurance, maintenance, taxes, staffing, etc...) continue to move up is a toxic combination for cash flow. Therefore, it is difficult to see how the delinquency rate on this debt does not continue to rise in 2026. Of note, while regional banks originate some 70% of CRE loans, they only own one-third of the CRE debt outstanding. That said, for three of four regional banks, CRE debt is the biggest share of their overall loan books.

3. More Cockroaches Appear In Private Credit

In recent months, some notable cracks have started to develop in the approximately $1.7 trillion private credit market. In the back half of September, there was the unexpected $2 billion bankruptcy of auto subprime lender TriColor Holdings, which was quickly followed by the $12 billion bankruptcy of auto parts supplier First Brands. These two bankruptcies resulted in significant write-offs from financial institutions like UBS, JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) and Jefferies Financial Group Inc. (JEF).

Earlier this month, Renovo Home Partners, a private equity–backed roll-up of home improvement businesses, filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy with up to $500 million in liabilities. More recently, Blue Owl Capital (OWL) blocked redemptions for investors that wanted to pull money from one of its longstanding private credit vehicles. The company did this as it tried to merge its $1.8 billion non-traded business development company, Blue Owl Capital Corporation II, with its much larger public counterpart, Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC), which manages just under $18 billion. A merger that management has now terminated.

This series of events put huge pressure not only on the stock of Blue Owl but also on other business development companies or BDCs. Jamie Dimon's observation that there is never one "cockroach" in the private credit markets in mid-October is starting to be prescient. This is as corporate bankruptcies in the U.S. are tracking to have their highest level in 15 years here in 2025.

After the Great Financial Crisis, new regulations made it harder for big banks to loan to risky creditors. That being said, the workaround to this new regulatory regime for the banks seems to be to lend to private equity and BDCs that, in turn, made loans to riskier parts of the economy. What possibly could go wrong?

And those are three potential significant headwinds I see developing in 2026 that are getting short shrift in the financial press.

data.title: Burlington Stores: Revenue Growth Is Slowing But Margin Expansion Keeps Me Bullish
data.description:
Burlington Stores, Inc. (BURL) is a fashion retailer in the US focused on selling apparel primarily, but also offering footwear and accessories, beauty products, and other home goods. The company has over 77,500 employees and over 1,200 locations. BURL is an off-price retailer, which means that it typically offers discounts up to 60% of regular retail price on its merchandise. BURL's total market cap is $15.6 billion, making it one of the larger firms in the retail industry and putting it solidly in the mid-cap category. BURL is one of the top ten constituents in the S&P MidCap 400 index and, thus, a top ten holding in the SPMD portfolio. The firm has recently updated investors on its performance in Q3 2025 and its outlook for Q4 2025, which has prompted me to re-evaluate my rating for the stock.

September 2024 Analysis

In my September 2024 article, I initiated a Buy rating on Burlington Stores based on the view that it was the strongest growth story in the off-price space. My thesis was supported by the following key factors:

  • Broad-based sales momentum was strengthening. BURL posted double-digit revenue growth and better-than-expected comps, driven by strong full-price sell-through and successful new store openings. These trends showed that demand for the brand was durable and that the company had multiple engines of top-line growth working at the same time.
  • Margins were improving for reasons that looked sustainable. Gross margin expanded sharply due to higher merchandise margins and lower freight costs, and EBIT margins jumped far more than management expected. The underlying mix shift toward full-price selling signaled better merchandising discipline, giving confidence that margin gains were not just cyclical noise.
  • Management’s guidance left room for repeated upside. Even after strong beats in Q1 and Q2, BURL kept its comp outlook for the back half of the year extremely cautious and built in headwinds from ocean freight. This conservative stance created a setup where the company could continue to exceed expectations on both sales and earnings.
  • The off-price model added resilience in a softening macro backdrop. As consumer uncertainty widened, value-oriented formats like BURL were attracting both lower-income and higher-income shoppers searching for better deals. That dynamic reduced downside cyclicality and supported the view that BURL could sustain growth even if broader apparel spending slowed.

I will now revisit the key points of my investment thesis, looking at the most recent earnings results and a brief note on the macro environment.

Q3 2025 Earnings

BURL reported the results of their operations in Q3 2025 on November 25th, which led to a rough response in trading during the day. The stock dropped -6.5% in pre-market trading, and the decline extended to a massive -12.2% in trading throughout the day. Investors turned harshly against the stock because its revenue numbers were notably weak in Q3 2025, with both total sales and comp sales estimates missed. BURL did report EPS estimates above Wall Street expectations, but this was largely ignored.

Revenue

In my initial analysis, strong revenue growth made me bullish on BURL’s ability to expand into new stores and sustain that top-line growth. Recent results have shown that the trend has turned around. In Q3 2025, total revenue increased 7.1% YoY to $2.71 billion, and comparable store sales were up just 1.0% YoY. Both of these figures were below estimates of total revenue at $2.72 billion and comp sales growth of 2.5% YoY. The annual growth in total revenue is the lowest annual growth rate reported for Q3 in three years and below the 9.3% YoY increase reported in Q2 2025. The outlook for Q4 2025 was also disappointing. Management said it expects comp sales growth between 0-2% YoY, weaker than the guidance of 1-2% YoY that Wall Street expected.

The only good news in these numbers is the reason behind them. CEO Michael O’Sullivan pointed out that the weak sales performance was caused by a decline in store traffic due to “unseasonably warm temperatures in our major markets.” Indeed, BURL (formerly known as Burlington Coat Factory) does have this seasonality factor in the Q3 period because it sees higher sales of fall and winter wear for the back-to-school season. The even better news here is that O’Sullivan noted that “comp trend then picked up to mid-single-digits in mid-October once the weather cooled, and that strong trend has continued through the first three weeks of November.” However, this has to be reconciled with the worse-than-expected guidance for Q4, and the optimism is limited.

Total revenue growth continues to be driven by BURL’s aggressive store expansion strategy. The company originally targeted about 100 net new stores per year from 2024 through 2028, and it is already ahead of that pace in 2025 with 103 net openings through the first three quarters. Management expects a quieter Q4, which puts full-year net additions at roughly 104 stores, in line with the long-term plan. While my earlier analysis highlighted how this expansion supported double-digit revenue gains in 2024, the 2025 results tell a slightly different story: overall sales are now rising at a mid-to-high single-digit rate, signaling that the marginal lift from each new store is beginning to moderate. This does not undermine the strategy, but it does suggest that BURL is transitioning from rapid footprint-driven growth toward a steadier phase where store productivity and merchandising execution will matter more than pure unit expansion.

Profitability

While the sales performance was a dim spot for BURL, the profitability measures were a bright spot. In Q3 2025, gross margin improved 30 bps from a year ago to 44.2%, with merchandise margins up 10 bps and freight expenses down -20 bps. BURL highlights that the cost of sales and SG&A expenses (which includes product sourcing costs) as a % of total revenue were both lower than a year ago, the former down -30 bps to 55.8% and the latter down -40 bps to 35.0%. All of this comes despite the retailer having to fight through the major headwind of tariff increases. CEO O’Sullivan made sure to highlight the impressive developments in margins, pointing out that these numbers are “at the high end of our updated 2025 margin guidance,” and BURL has “achieved 170 basis points of the 400 basis points of opportunity that we identified 2 years ago.”

The strength in Q3 2025 margins further lifted BURL’s profitability outlook. Management now expects adjusted EBIT margin expansion of 60 to 70 bps for the full year, a meaningful upgrade from the prior 20 to 40 bps range provided after Q2. This continued progress is especially notable given the sharp tariff hikes that took effect earlier in the year, a headwind that could have easily derailed merchandise margin and sourcing efficiency. Yet, investors largely looked past these improvements, even though the softness in comps appeared tied more to seasonality than to any deterioration in underlying demand. For me, the margin performance reinforces confidence in BURL’s operating model: the company is executing well on its cost structure and realizing the multiyear margin opportunity it outlined, even as its top line normalizes.

Macro Environment

Over the past year, the macro backdrop for retailers has grown meaningfully more complicated. The labor market has weakened considerably, with rising unemployment and softer job creation eroding the cushion that households enjoyed in 2023 and early 2024. Prices also remain a persistent concern for consumers, even as headline inflation has eased, and sentiment data continue to reflect pressure on real purchasing power. Despite that, consumption growth has stayed surprisingly strong: overall PCE is still running around the mid-5 percent range YoY ahead of the holiday season, and spending on clothing and footwear has accelerated more sharply than other nondurable categories even though apparel inflation is slightly negative on a YoY basis (apparel CPI -0.1% YoY in September). That combination of a weaker labor market but resilient discretionary spending keeps me cautious on the macro outlook, but it also underscores that value-oriented retailers like BURL are operating in an environment where demand remains intact even as household balance sheets grow more strained.

Relative to other players in the retail space, off-price retailers continue to benefit from a macro environment where value, price sensitivity, and trade-down behavior remain central to consumer decision-making. BURL’s Q3 commentary reinforced this advantage, especially among lower-income shoppers. As CEO O’Sullivan put it, “the headline is that we feel very good about the lower-income customer,” noting that these customers “have been very resilient” despite economic uncertainty and cost-of-living pressures. He highlighted that stores in lower-income trade areas “continue to outperform the chain,” a pattern BURL has seen for several quarters and one that other retailers are also reporting. This dynamic illustrates why the off-price channel is still positioned better than full-price apparel and department stores, particularly as shoppers trade down.

Peers

In my September 2024 report, I saw BURL as the best-in-class off-price retailer compared to its peers, the TJX Companies, Inc. (TJX) and Ross Stores, Inc. (ROST). Over the last year, the peer comparison has not changed much.

  • BURL’s recent slowdown in revenue growth puts it last in Q3 2025, but this is only the first time it wasn’t the strongest growing off-price retailer since Q1 2025. If we are looking at a short-term hit due to weather, BURL could retain its leading position in sales growth next quarter.

  • Looking at popular valuation metrics, BURL is on top in just about everyone. The key one to note is the significant gap in PEG ratios, suggesting that BURL’s margin expansions are fueling earnings growth that will allow for the stock price to increase through margin expansion. It is worth noting that these figures did come after the -12.2% drawdown after the earnings release.

Conclusion

Investors appeared to be in a tizzy over the slowdown in comp sales growth in Q3 2025 that was largely impacted by the weather. For some reason, they chose to ignore the strong margin expansion that came despite the tariff headwind and the bullish upgrade in the profitability outlook. I still believe that off-price retailers are the winners in the retail industry in the current macro environment, and BURL still offers the best opportunity in this niche segment. I am maintaining by Buy rating on the stock and see the 12.2% drawdown yesterday as an opportunity for investors to buy in at a discount.

data.title: Kohl's: Q3 Still Wasn't Good Enough
data.description:
Kohl's Corporation (KSS) reported the company’s fiscal Q3 results from the August-October period on the 25th of November. The department store operator showed a better financial performance than was expected, sending the stock surging nearly 43% for the day. I still believe that Kohl’s stock surge in on shaky grounds, mostly driven by a short squeeze – structural concerns remain in the business.

I maintained a Sell rating in my previous August article on the stock, titled “Kohl's Q2 Earnings: The Stock's Surge Is On Weak Grounds.” The stock has continued to outperform the market – Kohl’s has since returned 40%, well above the S&P 500’s (SP500) 4% return. The bearish thesis hasn’t worked out so far, but an increasingly expensive valuation makes the bearish thesis even stronger in my opinion.

Kohl’s Q3 Was Better Than Expected, But Structural Concerns Remain

Kohl’s reported Q3 financials well above expectations. The company’s $3.41 billion in revenues beat Wall Street’s consensus by $39 million, and the adjusted EPS of $0.10 showed an even better beat of $0.27. Coupled with the better-than-expected quarterly results, Kohl’s again raised the FY2025 guidance to a -2.5% to -3.0% comparable sales decline from -4% to -5% decline guided previously. The adjusted EPS guidance was raised significantly to $1.25-1.45 from $0.5-0.8 previously.

The report clearly sent positive signals – comparable store sales only declined by -1.7%, much better than Wall Street’s -3.9% consensus. The comparable sales decline is also well better than Kohl’s -3.2% year-to-date figure. The improvement was driven by acceleration in transactions while pricing stayed flat year-on-year. As maintaining sales volume in stores is critical to maintain profitability in retail, especially given Kohl’s incredibly thin margin profile, a beat in the metric is very meaningful. Kohl’s rollout of impulse queuing lines in September and simplified promotions seem to have contributed towards stabilizing sales, and healthy 2.4% growth in the digital channel also helped. The updated FY2025 guidance suggests around breakeven comparable sales for Q4.

Another clear positive in the report was especially good gross margin gains – the gross margin improved by 51 basis points to 39.6%, driven by improved inventory management and higher proprietary brand sales. Improving proprietary brand penetration is a very clear positive for Kohl’s margin outlook. Gross margin gains even accelerated from the 39 basis point year-to-date figure.

Regardless, I believe that Kohl’s long-term outlook remains weak. While improving comparable sales are clearly cause for some optimism, I believe that underlying structural concerns have persisted. The secular decline in department store traffic continues to weaken Kohl’s longer-term sales prospects. Kohl’s -1.7% comparable decline still very clearly underperformed Abercrombie & Fitch’s (ANF) 3% comparable growth, The Gap’s (GAP) 5% comparable growth, and Urban Outfitters’ (URBN) 8% comparable growth among others – weak consumer confidence clearly doesn’t explain the persisting sales weakness. The department store business model isn’t positioned well against other sales channels, most notably secular growth in ecommerce penetration. Kohl’s achieved modestly positive digital growth, but the company’s ecommerce positioning remains weak compared to much faster overall apparel ecommerce growth.

Weakening comparable sales caused 55 basis points of SG&A deleverage in Q3, causing Kohl’s adjusted operating income to decline to $77 million from $98 million a year ago despite gross margin gains – much more sales acceleration is still needed. Although a clear beat compared to Wall Street’s expectations, Kohl’s earnings still aren’t on solid grounds. The FY2025 guidance assumes the gross margin to improve by 30-35 basis points for the full year, suggesting that recent gross margin gains won’t repeat in Q4. At the same time, Kohl's comparable sales growth looks to remain below cost inflation.

Kohl’s Q3 improvements seem to be driven by successful operational changes, including the impulse queuing line rollout, successful changes in the promotional strategy, and a good proprietary brand strategy - the management has clearly succeeded at some level. Ultimately, such operational measures are only temporary against a secular decline; Kohl's needs to constantly find new factors to optimize. The company needs to show significantly better acceleration in FY2026 to even maintain its earnings level. High debt still makes Kohl’s future especially volatile.

Risks Outweigh Rewards in KSS’s Valuation

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Kohl’s. I slightly raised my revenue estimates, now suggesting that Kohl’s will achieve stagnant comparable sales over the long term with slow shrinkage in the store footprint. The assumption is reflected in -0.5% annual revenue growth.

I now assume the EBIT margin to stabilize at 0.5% instead of 0%, suggesting 2.3 percentage points of deleverage from FY2024 through SG&A deleverage. Kohl’s relatively low capital expenditures still make the company’s cash flow conversion outlook good.

The estimates put Kohl’s fair value at $13.8, suggesting 38% downside at the time of writing. I again believe the stock to carry a weak risk-to-reward, as further upside would require a significant change in Kohl’s earnings trajectory. The Q3 report doesn’t even suggest stabilization yet. A single DCF model is a very volatile valuation method due to Kohl’s high debt and earnings uncertainty, but the stock’s 14.5 forward P/E also underlines already too ambitious market expectations against a secular traffic decline.

The fair value estimate is up from $9.3 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 10.36%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 10% interest rate, in line with Kohl’s recent senior notes’ rate. I again assume quite a high 50% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.01% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.64. With a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 11.79% and the WACC at 10.36%.

Takeaway

I have previously underestimated Kohl's Corporation’s resilience, but I believe that underlying structural weaknesses remain in the business. The stock’s post-earnings surge seems to primarily reflect high 26% short interest instead of improving fundamentals, as comparable sales remain weak and earnings continue to decline. The stock’s massive gains continue to make the investment unattractive, as risks significantly outweigh potential rewards – I remain at a Sell rating for KSS stock despite the thesis working out poorly so far.

data.title: Energy Transfer: Owning The Infrastructure Behind A.I. Yielding Over 8%
data.description:
The A.I. revolution has created a massive amount of investment opportunities from the physical hardware to the companies deploying the most advanced software platforms. While many investors have gravitated toward NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Palantir (PLTR) I have been looking at the companies in the next layer that will experience ongoing benefits as more data centers come online. Energy Transfer (ET) has evolved from a traditional pipeline operator into one of the most important picks and shovels as they will act as a backbone for the A.I. revolution transporting increased amounts of energy throughout the country. There are surging power needs behind cloud computing and A.I. which will benefit ET’s nationwide network of over 130,000 miles of pipelines, storage and exporting facilities. I believe that ET offers investors with a rare combination of long-term growth with a growing distribution that currently yields over 8%. ET provides investors with direct exposure to the infrastructure that will keep data centers and A.I. clusters powered and I believe it’s undervalued at these levels. The energy sector isn’t going to grab headline but it’s critical to the buildout that is currently occurring and ET’s infrastructure can’t be replicated.

Following up on my previous article about Energy Transfer

ET has continued to fall since my last article was published in the middle of August (can be read here). Units of ET have fallen by -6.52% while the S&P 500 has increased by 4.45% since then and when the distribution is factored in ET’s total return is -4.64% since 8/15/25. I felt that the market would eventually start to reward ET as their infrastructure is critical to the A.I. revolution and its operational volumes moving through their system continues to rise. I felt that ET traded at a significant discount to its peer group and investors were getting rewarded through the 7.6% distribution yield to wait for a rebound. I am more bullish than I was as the Q3 numbers showed volumes across key segments hitting record levels and management securing long-dated contracts tied to power and data-center growth. Units of ET could take a while to rebound but as they bring more projects online to support the A.I. revolution their revenue and EBITDA should increase to support the distribution growth and the market should rerate them.

Q3 was a mix of underwhelming headlines but the business continued to get stronger

In Q3 ET reported $19.95 billion in revenue which was a decrease of -3.9% YoY while their net income came in at $1.02 billion compared to $1.18 billion in Q3 2024. Their Adjusted EBITDA declined YoY from $3.96 billion to $3.84 billion and while this could look like a story that’s cooling off, why the top and bottom line declined is more important that the actual decline. The softness in EBITDA was driven by factors that are either explicitly one-time or cyclical rather than structural. The intrastate natural gas segment saw a 30% drop in optimization margins which was roughly $99 million as they reflected tighter price spreads and fewer trading opportunities rather than a loss of physical throughput. ET absorbed roughly $60 million of non-recurring costs, including a $43 million prior-period ad valorem tax expense on the Rover pipeline system and one-time investigation and remediation costs in the NGL segment. These were impacts to the immediate earnings not impacts to the future numbers.

Q3 was on of ETs strongest quarters from a volume perspective. ET’s NGL transportation volumes were up 11% YoY while the volume in NGL and refined products terminals grew 10% YoY. The NGL export segment increased 13% YoY which set new records for ET. From a natural gas transportation aspect interstate increase 8% YoY while intrastate increased 5% YoY. ET’s midstream gathered volumes increased 3% YoY which was also a new record. This is why I continue to be bullish on ET as the demand for ET’s system is growing even when accounting rules can make the quarterly EBITDA look flat. This allowed ET to generate $1.9 billion in distributable cash flow (DCF) which made the DCF coverage ratio of the quarterly distribution come in at 1.66x. While there was slightly weaker headline financials due to optimization and one-time items ET experienced growth in volumes being transported throughout its system. In my opinion this is bullish for investors as it’s a recipe for durable income plus future unit price growth as the market turns on the energy sector.

ET is positioned to continue benefiting from the A.I. transformation that is occurring

ET’s business model is built on a fully integrated value chain from the wellhead to exporting facilities on the water which touches nearly every major U.S. producing basin and demand market. In Q3 ET’s Adjusted EBITDA was constructed by about 20% from midstream, 28% from NGL & refined products, 19% from crude oil, 17% from interstate/intrastate pipelines and storage, and 16% from SUN/USAC/other. ET’s EBITDA is secured by roughly 90% fee-based contracts with only 5–10% exposed to direct commodity price risk and 0–5% to spread/optimization. This diversification is critical as it allows ET to whether any landscape as no segment is responsible for an overwhelming amount of ET’s cash flow. ET’s profits are backed by long-term contracts and the expansive footprint allows ET to capture growth from LNG exports, petrochemical demand, and the growing data center demand for cloud and A.I. workloads.

The headlines on the financial news networks typically showcase the A.I. narrative through the lens of hyperscalers and chipmakers, but none of that compute happens without massive amounts of reliable electricity. ET’s gas network overlaps with both gas-fired power plants and existing and planned data centers across the country. Within just the last year ET as contracted over 6 Bcf per day of pipeline capacity under long-term agreements with a weighted average contract life of 18 years that are expected to generate more than $25 billion in fees. ET has executed multiple agreements with Oracle (ORCL) to supply roughly 900 MMcf/d of natural gas to three U.S. data centers. ET signed a 10-year agreement with Fermi America to provide a pipeline interconnection and exclusively supply approximately 300,000 MMBtu per day of natural gas to Fermi’s HyperGrid campus near Amarillo, Texas. ET also entered into a 20-year firm transportation deal to deliver 250,000 MMBtu per day of natural gas to Entergy Louisiana facilities to support baseload and peaking generation that will data-center and industrial demand.

As ET singes more contracts their backlog of projects looks even more attractive as it will be the future growth engine for the company. ET is expected to deploy $4.6 billion in CapEx during 2025 and has roughly $5 billion forecasted for 2026. To support the Permian midstream buildout ET is adding processing and treating capacity to increase processed volumes and feed ET’s own gas and NGL pipelines which will enhance its vertical integration. ET is expanding the Hugh Brinson Pipeline and Bethel storage to improve intrastate gas connectivity and storage which will address the growing power demand from data centers. At Nederland and Marcus Hook, ET is expanding refrigerated storage and export capacity as these projects increase ET’s NGL export capacity. I believe that ET’s backlog will provide attractive returns on invested capital.

When I look at the CapEx buildout from Alphabet (GOOGL), META Platforms (META), Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft (MSFT) it doesn’t seem like they are slowing down anytime soon with their data center expansion which is bullish for ET. In 2024 the combination of these 4 companies allocated $217.27 billion toward CapEx which was an increase of $77.14 billion (55%) YoY. On a YTD basis their combined CapEx allocation increased by 51.78% ($112.49 billion) to $329 billion and they are expected to finish 2025 having spent $354 billion on CapEx. The A.I. expansion isn’t stopping and the new infrastructure will end continuous energy which is bullish for ET.

The overwhelming driver for the increased CapEx META, AMZN, GOOGL, and MSFT from is data-center and AI infrastructure. The commentary from their earnings calls indicates that more than 50% is being allocate dto the data center stack which includes the buildings, power and cooling, GPUs/servers, and networking that support A.I. workloads. AMZN just announced a $50 billion deal to boost A.I. and supercomputing infrastructure for the U.S. government. This is expected to break ground in 2026 and will add nearly 1.3 gigawatts of A.I. and supercomputing capacity by building data centers with advanced compute and networking technologies. All of this CapEx is being funneled directly into data centers and A.I. compute which is why power grids, gas demand, and midstream infrastructure are becoming more critical to Big Tech. ET has already announced roughly 1.0 Bcf/d of firm gas capacity explicitly tied to A.I. data centers and I believe that the increased data-center energy bills will show up as long-duration fee-based volumes through ET’s infrastructure.

Energy Transfer still looks undervalued compared to its peers

When I compare ET to Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Plains All American (PAA), and MPLX LP (MPLX), I still see a lot of value left to be unlocked. When I look at how the market is valuing ET based on the amount of Adjusted EBITDA it produces, I feel as if there is still a major opportunity here. ET trades at 8.47 times its Adjusted EBITDA when looking at its enterprise value, which is the lowest multiple in its peer group. The peer group average is 9.89 times, and ET is the only one that trades at less than 9 times its Adjusted EBITDA. When I look at the Adjusted EBITDA to market cap valuation, it's the same story as ET trades at 3.56 times its Adjusted EBITDA compared to a peer group average of 5.71 times. ET has the lowest valuation in both categories of its peer group.

When I look at the amount of DCF ET is generating, it trades at 6.37 times its DCF, which is the 2nd lowest in the peer group. The peer group average is 7.80 times, while ET is trading at 6.37 times its DCF. When I look at these profitability metrics, I believe there is a case to be made that ET is undervalued. ET is being undervalued on the most important profitability metrics for midstream operators and there is a lot of value to be unlocked.

CME Group is now expecting that there is a 84.3% chance that the Fed cuts rates by 25 bps at the December FOMC meeting. In a rate environment where the Fed effective rate is declining and the risk-free rate of return is falling ET’s distributing yield will look more enticing. Currently ET has a yield of 8.17% which is the 2nd largest in the peer group. While units of ET has declined I believe investors are getting paid to wait with a distribution that continues to grow.

ET is expecting to generate between $16.1–$16.5 billion in Adjusted EBITDA during the 2025 fiscal year which signals further growth from the $15.5 billion it generated in 2024. For investors who think in terms of yield plus growth ET is trading at an attractive valuation with an 8%+ cash yield backed by fee-based infrastructure with a goal from the management team of growing it's profitability to sustain 3-5% annual distribution growth. The combination of EPD, MPLX, PAA, and ET trade at an average of 9.89 times their enterprise value based on the Adjusted EBITDA they generate which takes debt into consideration. When I look at the average market cap multiple they trade at on Adjusted EBITDA they trade at 5.71 times. ET is being deeply discounted as it trades at 8.47x Adjusted EBITDA and 3.56x Adjusted EBITDA based on its EV and market cap. I arrive at a fair value of $19.51 by increasing ET's market cap to where it still trades at the lower end of the range in the peer group to be conservative. If ET traded at a $67 billion market cap it would still have the lowest EV to Adjusted EBITDA ratio in the peer group at 4.27x and the lowest market cap to Adjusted EBITDA ratio at 8.47x. This would increase the unit price 19.94% to $19.51 which I feel is fair value without assigning a premium on the business due to the scale of DCF and Adjusted EBITDA being produced.

Risks to investing in Energy Transfer

am very bullish on ET, but there are several risk factors for investors to consider, and everyone should do their own due diligence. ET has over $63 billion in long-term debt on its balance sheet which makes them vulnerable to unfavorable macroeconomic environments, especially if the Fed needs to raise interest rates. If ET’s cost of capital increases it could negatively impact their profitability model. ET operates in a heavily regulated sector, and lawsuits over environmental permits or unexpected failures of its pipelines could negatively impact their operations. If the data center buildout slows down sooner than expected ET’s backlog of projects may not be as valuable as they could be as the need for energy could tapper off. ET is vulnerable to several aspects outside of their control and investors should do their own research.

Conclusion

Units of ET have fell by almost -17% over the past year and the reality is that they can go lower before rebounding. I believe fair value for ET is at least $19.51 per unit which is a 19.94% increase from where it trades today which would put its market cap at $67 billion. At a $67 billion market cap ET would still have the lowest EV to Adjusted EBITDA ratio in the peer group at 4.27x and the lowest market cap to Adjusted EBITDA ratio at 8.47x. I think that over the next year we could see ET trade over $20 per unit as it is deeply discounted based off its profitability and as the A.I. expansion continues to increase its infrastructure will become more valuable. As the Fed lowers interest rates, ET should get some reprieve on its current debt load, and the lower cost of capital could put them in a position where margins expand due to lower interest expenses. I am planning to add to ET as I see a lot of value to be unlocked while I sit back and collect the large distributions.

data.title: argenx: Sights Set On $1,000
data.description:
When they are doing well, some of the investments and opportunities are equal parts beautiful and frustrating. argenx (ARGX) is such a case. Its growth story has been, for the most part, beautiful to observe, and yet frustrating because of how often the stock looked expensive, and that it is because it keeps significantly outperforming Street expectations.

The most frustrating part is getting the magnitude of opportunity and outperformance wrong. My goal is not to make conservative or aggressive estimates, but to be as accurate as possible. And looking back, it is amazing how well argenx executed since launching Vyvgart, initially for generalized myasthenia gravis ('gMG'), and last year for chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy ('CIDP').

In February 2024, I said that we could see 2025 net sales of Vyvgart reach or exceed $3 billion versus the $2.4 billion consensus at the time, and I subsequently raised my estimates to $3.6 billion and $3.7 billion this year, respectively, while still seeing room for outperformance.

Fast forward another six months, and argenx is on track to deliver more than $4.1 billion in net sales of Vyvgart in 2025.

And yes, the stock looks expensive now, again. It almost always does. But I remain bullish on the company’s growth prospects, and expect the value creation to be more diversified going forward with empasiprubart’s clinical program maturing, and with positive cash flows opening up business development as another avenue for growth going forward.

Argenx maintains commercial momentum in 2025

Back in May, I noted how the revenue outperformance at the start of the year was not good enough to push the stock higher at the time, but that there was no reason for concern. I even increased my full-year net sales estimate for Vyvgart from $3.6 billion+ to $3.7 billion+.

Since then, argenx delivered two quarters of exceptional growth. Vyvgart has accomplished the rare feat of growth re-acceleration with Y/Y growth rates improving from the mid-70s and low 80s in the first three quarters of 2024 to the high 90s in the last four quarters.

This was achieved through the very impressive launch for the treatment of CIDP with more than 2,500 patients treated globally, and the recent launches of the prefilled syringe (‘PFS’) version of Vyvgart Hytrulo (the subcutaneous version). And there are no signs of the momentum waning.

One complaint I have is how U.S. centric the growth has been since launch with 85% of net sales in the third quarter coming from the U.S. I would like to see improved traction in other territories going forward.

The pipeline should deliver five registrational readouts in 2026

On the pipeline side this year, there was only one notable and successful readout of Vyvgart in seronegative gMG patients with an sBLA application to the FDA expected in the following weeks. Argenx expects that the expanded label will add approximately 11,000 seronegative gMG patients in the U.S. versus approximately 17,000 patients addressable at launch. The seronegative population should become a decent growth driver in 2027, and beyond.

And while there were no other significant readouts this year, the pipeline is building considerable momentum. There are currently 10 active phase 3 trials and 5 should generate topline results in 2026.

I expect at least three of these trials to report positive data – Vyvgart in ocular myasthenia gravis and the three inflammatory myopathies (in a single trial), and empasiprubart in multifocal motor neuropathy ('MMN').

Success is not guaranteed, but the reasons for optimism are Vyvgart working well in myasthenia gravis (now both seronegative and positive patients) and generating positive results in inflammatory myopathies at the interim analysis last year, and empasiprubart generating strong efficacy in the phase 2 trial in MMN patients.

I am not that sure about the thyroid eye disease (‘TED’) and immune thrombocytopenia ('ITP') readouts of Vyvgart. There is not sufficient evidence of activity of anti-FcRn antibodies (and none from Vyvgart itself with argenx relying on a small dataset from a competing anti-FcRn therapy) and if Vyvgart succeeds, whether it will be competitive to an IGF-1 antibody like Tepezza.

Regarding the ITP trial, there is evidence that Vyvgart works and it is approved for ITP in Japan, but the previous phase 3 trials delivered mixed outcomes that did not merit consideration of other regulators.

I would not write off either trial, and do think there is a greater than 50% probability of success, I am just saying the available data do not provide a high level of confidence in a positive outcome.

However, even three of five positive readouts should result in a significantly improved growth outlook for 2027 and the following years.

We should also start seeing some early-stage data from new compounds. I would not expect the results to have a big impact on argenx’s share price next year, but the phase 1 results of ARGX-213 (the next-generation, longer acting anti-FcRn candidate) will be an important event for the long-term outlook of the anti-FcRn franchise. To be relevant for argenx, ARGX-213 would need to deliver similar IgG lowering to Vyvgart and have the potential for once monthly dosing versus weekly dosing for Vyvgart.

On the negative side, there was some pipeline attrition this year:

  • Vyvgart did not generate a positive signal in a phase 2 trial in lupus nephritis patients.
  • Development of empasiprubart in dermatomyositis was discontinued due poor study enrollment.

Argenx is starting to generate significant cash flows – business development as another growth avenue going forward

Argenx has achieved profitability on a quarterly basis this year and has added nearly $1 billion in cash to the balance sheet in the first three quarters of the year.

With revenue growth expected to significantly outpace expense growth, we should see argenx deliver increasing profits and cash flows in the following quarters and years, and this adds business development as another avenue for long-term growth.

The good news is that, with significant late-stage pipeline activity, new early-stage projects, and continued strong commercial growth prospects, there is no urgency to do deals and management can take the time to properly evaluate additional external opportunities.

Conclusion

Argenx investors have their sights set on the $1,000 per share milestone. The stock has been a strong performer since it came public and has largely neglected the poor industry sentiment since the 2021 top for biotech stocks and it has recently benefited from the resurgence of biotech stocks. Below is the share price performance since my first public article on Seeking Alpha in February 2019 versus biotech ETFs IBB and XBI.

The valuation is on the high side if we look at the EV/revenue ratio, but so are the growth rates, and the long-term outlook is both strong and improving with business development as another way to strengthen it.

The risks in the near-term are industry sentiment shifts, and company-related developments such as clinical trial failures next year, or strong results from competitors in and outside of the anti-FcRn class.

Argenx has so far enjoyed very little in-class competition in the gMG market and none in the CIDP market, but both should change in the following years with a potential but probably modest impact from the recent launch of Johnson & Johnson’s (JNJ) Imaavy (nipocalimab), and competition outside of the anti-FcRn class in gMG is about to increase in both the near- and the long-term with the expected approval of Amgen’s (AMGN) CD19 antibody Uplizna next month, and Regeneron (REGN) will submit an application for approval of C5 siRNA candidate cemdisiran next year. Both products have demonstrated competitive efficacy and have the convenience of twice annual (Uplizna) and quarterly dosing (cemdisiran).

data.title: Triple Flag: A Precious Metals Compounder At A Reasonable Price
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground. MTPA = million tonnes per annum. FS/DFS = Definitive Feasibility Study.

Q3 Production & Sales

Triple Flag Precious Metals Corp. (TFPM) released its Q3 '25 results earlier this month, reporting quarterly sales of ~27,000 gold-equivalent ounces, a 9% decline from the year-ago period.

Lower GEO sales was related to planned lower grades at Fosterville as ultra high-grade Swan Zone reserves have been mined out, lower contribution from Cerro Lindo, no contribution from ATO while Steppe Gold remains in default of its delivery obligations under its gold/silver stream and lower contributions from Agbaou/Bonikro in Cote d'Ivoire, where the prior period benefited from ounces in the January 1st through August 14th (up to date of acquisition closing for the Bonikro/Agbaou streams) vs. solely the typical quarterly period.

In summary, while Q3 '25 was a down quarter for GEO sales, much of this can be explained purely by:

  1. lapping difficult comps from the prior period at Bonikro/Agbaou which benefited from 9 months of sales vs. a single quarter.
  2. one of its partners, Steppe Gold, deciding to go rogue despite Triple Flag being a supportive lender at a time when it needed it at the bottom of the cycle (2017).

And if we separate out these two unusual items (~4,000 GEOs related to Bonikro/Agbaou excess deliveries from H1'24 and ATO withheld deliveries), Triple Flag's GEO sales were actually up year-over-year.

As for its quarterly contribution by asset, Northparkes (54% gold stream, 80% silver stream) was the most significant contributor at ~10,600 GEOs. This represented a record contribution from this Australian copper-gold asset, benefiting from higher-grade gold ore from the E31 and E31N pits. To be clear, mining from these higher grade pits was completed in Q1 of this year, but stockpiled ore processed will continue to benefit stream deliveries through year-end.

Just as importantly, Evolution (OTCPK:CAHPF) noted that cave performance exceeded expectations in fiscal Q1 '26 (calendar Q3 '25), and with solid net mine cash flow of ~$195 million in its last fiscal year, this supports continued investment in this asset.

That said, we will see more normalized gold production levels from Northparkes going forward with these two pits now mined out. In fact, gold production is expected to halve year-over-year based on guidance, with Evolution guiding for 25,000 ounces of gold at the top end in FY2026, down from ~49,000 ounces produced in FY2025.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

"I'll just open it by saying that the lower gold production is linked predominantly to E31 and that is that there were 2 pits there. The gold dominant one was mainly processed in '25. And then the stockpiles, which will be the lower grade will come through in '26 and it won't be for the full year."

- Evolution Mining FY2025 Results Conference Call.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

On the exploration front, drilling continues to support significant resource growth outside of future block caves, with Major Tom and E51 continuing to deliver solid near-surface copper-gold hits. Highlight intercepts from Major Tom included 171 meters at 0.38% copper and 0.06 G/T of gold and 116 meters at 0.335 copper and 0.05 G/T of gold. Meanwhile, E51 drilling yielded an intercept of 84 meters at 0.43% copper and 0.13 G/T of gold with room to fill in the areas between these two deposits.

Overall, this is great news for Triple Flag, especially given that this key asset (#1 contributor) already boasts a 30+ year mine life, providing visibility into deliveries coming to Triple Flag well into the 2050s from this streaming contract.
